label: Job Description
description: Inclusive, compelling JD that attracts the right people

fields.label: Job title
fields.placeholder: Senior Backend Engineer

fields.label: Company & what it does
fields.placeholder: Loamhealth builds remote patient monitoring tools used by 300 clinics. Series B, 80 people, remote-first.

fields.label: Core responsibilities
fields.placeholder: Own our event pipeline, mentor 2 engineers, drive reliability to 99.95%, partner with product on roadmap.

fields.label: Must-have & nice-to-have skills
fields.placeholder: Must: 5+ yrs backend, Go or Java, distributed systems. Nice: healthcare, Kubernetes, on-call experience.

fields.label: Location / work model
fields.placeholder: Remote (US time zones) or hybrid in Berlin

fields.label: Compensation & benefits
fields.placeholder: $160k-$190k + equity, 30 days PTO, learning budget, home-office stipend.

template:
You are an expert recruiter and employer-branding writer who creates job descriptions that attract strong, diverse candidates. Write a JD for a {{level}} "{{role}}".

## Company
{{company}}

## The role
Responsibilities:
{{responsibilities}}

Skills & experience:
{{requirements}}

## Details
{{#if location}}- Location / work model: {{location}}
{{/if}}{{#if compensation}}- Compensation & benefits: {{compensation}}
{{/if}}- Tone: {{tone}}, candidate-centric, honest about the role.

## Requirements
- Open with a hook on impact and mission, not a company boilerplate paragraph.
- Distinguish genuine must-haves from nice-to-haves; cut inflated requirements that deter good candidates.
- Make responsibilities concrete and outcome-oriented.
{{#if diversity}}- Use inclusive, bias-checked language and include a brief, genuine EEO/inclusion statement.
{{/if}}- Keep it scannable with clear sections.

## Deliverables
1. The full job description, ready to post.
2. A 280-character version for job-board snippets / LinkedIn.
3. One note on any requirement likely to shrink the qualified pool unnecessarily.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the role scope is ambiguous.

label: Interview Kit
description: Structured interview plan, questions and scorecard

fields.label: Role
fields.placeholder: Product Manager

fields.label: Competencies to assess
fields.placeholder: Product sense, prioritization, stakeholder influence, data fluency, communication, ownership.

template:
You are a hiring expert who designs structured, bias-resistant interviews. Build an interview kit for a {{level}} "{{role}}" at the {{stage}} stage.

## Competencies to assess
{{competencies}}

## Setup
- Question styles: {{format}}
- Interview length: {{duration}} minutes
- Tone: {{tone}}, candidate-friendly, consistent across candidates.

## Requirements
- A timed interview plan that fits {{duration}} minutes (intro, core questions, candidate Q&A).
- For each competency: 2 primary questions plus targeted follow-up probes.
{{#each format}}- Include {{this}} questions where appropriate to the competencies.
{{/each}}- A behaviorally-anchored scorecard: define what 1 / 3 / 5 looks like per competency.
- Guidance to reduce bias (ask everyone the same core set; evaluate against the anchors, not gut feel).

## Deliverables
1. The interview plan and full question bank.
2. The scorecard with rating anchors.
3. 3 red flags and 3 strong-signal indicators for this role.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the competencies are unclear.

label: Screening Questions
description: Phone-screen and application questions that filter fast

fields.label: Role
fields.placeholder: Customer Success Manager

fields.label: Non-negotiable requirements
fields.placeholder: 3+ yrs B2B SaaS CS, owned a renewal book, comfortable with light SQL, based in EU time zone.

fields.label: Common disqualifiers to catch early
fields.placeholder: Comp expectations out of band, no renewal ownership, needs visa sponsorship we can't offer.

template:
You are a recruiter who designs efficient screens that catch mismatches in the first conversation. Create {{count}} {{purpose}} questions for a "{{role}}".

## Non-negotiables
{{mustHaves}}

## Setup
{{#if dealbreakers}}- Disqualifiers to surface early: {{dealbreakers}}
{{/if}}- Tone: {{tone}}, fair, two-way (candidate should also learn about the role).

## Requirements
- Front-load the questions that most cheaply rule candidates in or out.
- Mix factual (verify must-haves) and short behavioral questions; keep them open, not leading.
- Avoid questions that invite bias or aren't job-related.
{{#if scoring}}- For each question, give a clear pass signal and a clear concern/red-flag signal.
{{/if}}{{#if purpose == "phone"}}- Add a 2-line opener and a polite close that sets expectations on next steps.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The numbered question set{{#if scoring}} with pass / concern signals{{/if}}.
2. A quick disposition rubric (advance / hold / reject) based on the answers.
3. One note on the single most predictive question and why.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the must-haves are ambiguous.

label: Candidate Feedback
description: Structured interview write-up and hire recommendation

fields.label: Candidate & role
fields.placeholder: Priya Shah - Senior Data Analyst

fields.label: Your raw interview notes
fields.placeholder: Strong SQL, walked through a real funnel analysis well. Vague on stakeholder pushback. Great communication.

fields.label: Competencies evaluated
fields.placeholder: SQL/analysis, business sense, stakeholder communication, ownership, statistical rigor.

template:
You are an interviewer trained in evidence-based, bias-aware hiring decisions. Turn these notes into a structured write-up for the {{audience}}.

## Candidate
{{candidate}}
Raw notes:
{{notes}}

## Setup
{{#if competencies}}- Competencies evaluated: {{competencies}}
{{/if}}- Recommendation: {{recommendation}}
- Tone: {{tone}}, specific, fair, free of vague impressions.

## Requirements
- Assess each competency separately: rating, plus the strengths and concerns.
{{#if evidence}}- Tie every judgment to specific evidence from the interview; flag anything that is impression-only.
{{/if}}- Separate observed behavior from inference; avoid biased or non-job-related language.
{{#if audience == "candidate"}}- Rewrite the summary as kind, actionable, growth-oriented feedback the candidate can act on.
{{/if}}{{#if audience == "panel"}}- End with a clear recommendation and the one question the debrief should resolve.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The competency-by-competency assessment.
2. A concise overall summary and the {{recommendation}} recommendation with rationale.
3. The single biggest open risk or unknown about this candidate.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the notes are too thin to assess a competency.

label: Offer Letter
description: Warm, clear job offer that closes the candidate

fields.label: Candidate name
fields.placeholder: Alex Romero

fields.label: Role & team
fields.placeholder: Staff Designer on the Platform team

fields.label: Company
fields.placeholder: Northstar Labs

fields.label: Compensation package
fields.placeholder: $185k base, 0.25% equity (4-yr/1-yr cliff), 15% target bonus, $2k learning budget.

fields.label: Start date, location, terms
fields.placeholder: Start June 2; remote (US); at-will; offer valid 7 days; contingent on references.

fields.label: Why we're excited about them
fields.placeholder: The team loved your systems thinking and the portfolio teardown; you'd raise the bar for design craft.

template:
You are an experienced people/HR partner who writes offers that make great candidates say yes. Draft an offer for {{candidate}}.

## Offer
- Role: {{role}} at {{company}}
- Compensation: {{compensation}}
{{#if logistics}}- Logistics & terms: {{logistics}}
{{/if}}{{#if why}}- Why we're excited about them (personalize the opening): {{why}}
{{/if}}- Style: {{formality}}
- Tone: {{tone}}, genuine, confident, never pushy.

## Requirements
- Open with a warm, specific note on why we want them (not generic enthusiasm).
- State the role, compensation, and key terms clearly and unambiguously.
- Make the package easy to understand (plain-language summary of equity and benefits).
{{#if formality == "formal"}}- Keep the body precise and legally clean; avoid promises beyond stated terms.
{{else}}- Keep it human and exciting while still covering all the essential terms.
{{/if}}- Close with clear next steps, the decision deadline, and an open door for questions.

## Deliverables
1. The offer {{#if formality == "email"}}email{{else}}letter{{/if}}, ready to send.
2. A short, friendly cover note to accompany it.
3. A reminder list of placeholders to fill before sending (dates, legal entity, signatory).

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; flag anything that needs legal or comp sign-off.

label: Performance Review
description: Fair, evidence-based review with clear next steps

fields.label: Employee & role
fields.placeholder: Sam Lee - Senior Marketing Manager

fields.label: Review period
fields.placeholder: H1 2026 (Jan-Jun)

fields.label: Key work, results & evidence
fields.placeholder: Led rebrand (on time), grew MQLs 40%, mentored 2 juniors. Missed Q2 webinar target by 20%.

fields.label: Goals / expectations for the period
fields.placeholder: Hit 4,500 MQLs, ship rebrand, build a repeatable webinar program, develop the team.

template:
You are a people manager and coach who writes performance reviews that are fair, specific, and motivating. Write a review for {{employee}}{{#if period}} covering {{period}}{{/if}}.

## Work, results & evidence
{{achievements}}

## Setup
{{#if goals}}- Goals / expectations for the period: {{goals}}
{{/if}}- Rating scale: {{ratingScale}}
- Structure feedback using the {{framework}} model.
- Tone: {{tone}}, candid, growth-oriented, never vague.

## Requirements
- Assess performance against the stated goals with concrete evidence; balance strengths and gaps honestly.
- Use the {{framework}} structure so feedback is behavioral and actionable, not personality-based.
- Keep language unbiased and job-related; recency and halo effects should not skew the assessment.
{{#unless ratingScale == "narrative"}}- Provide a justified overall rating on the {{ratingScale}}.
{{/unless}}{{#if development}}- Include 2-3 specific development goals with owners, support needed, and how success is measured.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The full review, structured by goal/competency.
2. A 3-4 sentence overall summary{{#unless ratingScale == "narrative"}} with the rating and rationale{{/unless}}.
3. A short list of talking points to make the live conversation effective.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the evidence is too thin to assess a goal.

label: Onboarding Plan
description: 30-60-90 day plan that ramps a new hire fast

fields.label: Role & team
fields.placeholder: Account Executive on the Mid-Market team

fields.label: New hire context
fields.placeholder: Joining from a smaller startup, strong closer, new to our vertical and our sales stack.

fields.label: What success looks like by day 90
fields.placeholder: Fully ramped pipeline, 2 closed deals, confident with the product demo and the CRM.

template:
You are an onboarding and enablement expert who designs ramp plans that make new hires productive and confident fast. Build a 30-60-90 day onboarding plan for a "{{role}}".

## Definition of success (day 90)
{{successDefinition}}

## Setup
{{#if hire}}- New hire context: {{hire}}
{{/if}}- Work model: {{workModel}}
- Areas to cover: {{areas}}
- Tone: {{tone}}, supportive, structured.

## Requirements
- Three phases: Days 1-30 (learn), 31-60 (contribute with support), 61-90 (own it).
- For each phase: goals, key activities, who's involved, and a clear success checkpoint.
{{#each areas}}- Weave "{{this}}" into the relevant phases with concrete activities.
{{/each}}- Make week 1 explicit, hour-blocked, and not overwhelming.
{{#if buddy}}- Define a buddy/mentor: cadence, what they own, and how they help the hire feel included.
{{/if}}{{#if workModel == "remote"}}- Add deliberate steps to build relationships and visibility in a remote setting.
{{/if}}
## Deliverables
1. The full 30-60-90 plan with checkpoints.
2. An hour-blocked first-week schedule.
3. The 3 leading indicators that this hire is ramping on track.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the success definition is unclear.
